exercises.title: MLM vs next-token: what BERT predicts
exercises.prompt: Explain the masked language modeling objective: what gets masked, what the model predicts, and why bidirectional context is both BERT's superpower and the reason it can't generate text the way GPT does.
exercises.hint: Mask ~15% of tokens; predict the originals from left AND right context. Bidirectional = great representations, but no autoregressive generation.
exercises.solution:
**The MLM objective:** randomly select ~**15% of tokens** in a sentence and replace them (mostly with a special `[MASK]`), then train the encoder to **predict the original token** at each masked position using the **full surrounding context — both left and right**. Loss is cross-entropy only on the masked positions.

**Why bidirectionality is the superpower:** to fill 'the [MASK] sat on the mat', the model uses words on *both* sides. This produces **deeply contextual representations** of every token — the same word gets different embeddings in different contexts — which is exactly what downstream *understanding* tasks (classification, NER, QA, retrieval) want. You fine-tune one small head on top and inherit 'understands English'.

**Why it can't generate like GPT:** generation is autoregressive — produce token `t` from tokens `< t` only. But BERT was trained to peek at the **future** (right context), so it has no notion of 'predict the next token given only the past'. Its attention is non-causal; ask it to generate left-to-right and it has never learned that distribution. BERT *understands*; GPT *generates*. One sentence of difference in the objective, two different families of model.

exercises.title: The 80/10/10 masking recipe
exercises.prompt: Implement BERT's masking strategy: of the 15% chosen positions, 80% become [MASK], 10% become a random token, and 10% are left unchanged. Explain the train/inference mismatch this recipe is designed to fix and why each of the three cases matters.
exercises.hint: [MASK] never appears at fine-tuning/inference, so training only on [MASK] would create a distribution shift. Random + unchanged tokens force the model to build good reps for ALL tokens, not just masked slots.
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np

MASK_ID = 103  # special [MASK] token id (example)

def bert_mask(token_ids, vocab_size, mask_prob=0.15, rng=None):
    rng = rng or np.random.default_rng(0)
    ids = token_ids.copy()
    labels = np.full_like(ids, -100)        # -100 = ignore in the loss
    n = len(ids)
    chosen = rng.random(n) < mask_prob       # the ~15%
    for i in np.where(chosen)[0]:
        labels[i] = token_ids[i]             # predict the ORIGINAL token
        r = rng.random()
        if r < 0.8:
            ids[i] = MASK_ID                 # 80% -> [MASK]
        elif r < 0.9:
            ids[i] = rng.integers(vocab_size)# 10% -> random token
        # else 10% -> leave unchanged
    return ids, labels

x = np.array([5, 22, 91, 7, 64, 33, 12, 88])
masked, labels = bert_mask(x, vocab_size=30000)
print("input :", masked)
print("labels:", labels)   # only chosen positions are != -100
```

**The mismatch it fixes:** `[MASK]` is a training-only artifact — it **never appears at fine-tuning or inference**, where the model sees real, complete sentences. If 100% of masked positions were `[MASK]`, the model would learn 'only produce a prediction where I see `[MASK]`' and would behave oddly on the clean inputs it actually faces (a train/serve distribution shift).

**Why each case:**
- **80% `[MASK]`** — the core fill-in-the-blank signal.
- **10% random token** — forces the model to *not blindly trust the observed token*; it must use context to decide whether a token even fits, building robustness and good reps for corrupted input.
- **10% unchanged** — the model must still predict the correct token for positions that look totally normal, so it learns **useful contextual representations for every token**, not just for slots marked `[MASK]`. Together, 10%+10% mean the model can't tell *which* positions are being scored just from seeing `[MASK]`, so it has to represent the whole sentence well — which is the point.

exercises.title: Pretrain-then-finetune and what came after BERT
exercises.prompt: BERT's lasting contribution was the pretrain-once / finetune-many paradigm. Explain why this was a revelation in 2018, how fine-tuning reuses the pretrained encoder, and where encoder-only MLM models still win in 2026 versus where decoder-only LLMs took over. Mention one improvement a successor (RoBERTa/ELECTRA) made.
exercises.hint: Before BERT every task trained from scratch on its own labels. Fine-tuning swaps a small task head onto frozen-ish pretrained weights. Encoders still rule embeddings/classification; LLMs took generation. ELECTRA replaced MLM with replaced-token detection (more efficient).
exercises.solution:
**Why it was a revelation (2018):** before BERT, *every* NLP task — sentiment, NER, QA, entailment — trained its **own model from scratch on its own labeled data**. There was no reusable 'understands English' checkpoint. BERT pretrained one transformer encoder on **all the unlabeled text on the internet** (via MLM + next-sentence prediction), then you **fine-tune** it on your small labeled dataset. Suddenly tasks with a few thousand labels could ride on representations learned from billions of words — a massive **parameter- and data-efficiency** jump. Within ~18 months BERT variants topped every NLP leaderboard.

**How fine-tuning reuses the encoder:**
```text
[pretrained BERT encoder]  ->  [CLS]/token outputs  ->  small task head (e.g. Linear)
        (reused weights)                                 (new, task-specific)
```
You attach a thin head (a linear classifier on the `[CLS]` representation for sentence tasks, or per-token for NER) and train end-to-end with a **small learning rate**, nudging the pretrained weights rather than learning from zero. The expensive 'understand language' part is amortized once.

**2026 division of labor:**
- **Encoder-only MLM models still win** where you need **cheap, fast, high-quality representations of a fixed input**: **embeddings for retrieval/semantic search**, reranking, classification, NER, content moderation. They're small, bidirectional, and you run them once per document — no generation needed. Modern variants (e.g. embedding models, ModernBERT) keep this niche alive.
- **Decoder-only LLMs took over** anything **generative or instruction-following**: chat, summarization, code, agents — and increasingly do classification too via prompting. The pretrain→finetune idea itself **generalized** into pretrain→instruction-tune→RLHF for LLMs.

**A successor improvement:** **RoBERTa** showed BERT was undertrained and dropped next-sentence prediction (train longer, bigger batches, dynamic masking) for a big gain with the same architecture. **ELECTRA** replaced MLM with **replaced-token detection** — a small generator swaps in plausible tokens and the main model classifies *every* token as original-or-replaced, so the loss covers 100% of positions instead of just 15%, making pretraining **far more compute-efficient** for the same quality.
